title: Barge-in detection — allow interrupting robot while it speaks
description:
Allow users to interrupt the robot mid-speech, while ignoring background noise.

## Key finding: XMOS XVF3800 has built-in AEC
The hardware echo cancellation already removes the robot's own voice from the mic signal. Any speech detected during playback is genuinely from the environment. This makes barge-in detection much simpler.

## Architecture:
1. Replace blocking sd.play()+sd.wait() with non-blocking sd.OutputStream callback
2. Monitor mic via VAD while audio plays (main thread, not a new thread)
3. Classify interrupt: duration gate (480ms sustained speech) + DOA (front hemisphere)
4. On confirmed barge-in: stop playback, capture remaining speech, transcribe, process as new turn

## Implementation plan:
1. New app/interruptible_player.py — OutputStream callback + VAD monitoring
2. Modify audio_io.py — share VAD model, add record_from_buffer()
3. Modify agent_client.py — add inject_interruption() for partial context
4. Modify orchestrator.py — replace play calls with interruptible player
5. Modify main.py — wire up shared VAD and player

## Classification (cheapest first):
- Duration gate: 480ms sustained VAD (filters coughs, brief sounds)
- DOA gate: front hemisphere only (filters side conversations)
- Energy gate: minimum RMS threshold
- NOT using semantic classifier (too slow for barge-in, 250-550ms)

## What happens on interrupt:
- Stop OutputStream immediately
- Reset wobbler
- Capture user's speech (already buffered from VAD monitoring)
- Track partial response for LLM context
- Process as new conversation turn

## Estimated: ~300 lines across 3-4 files

title: CPU spike causes audio to stop — face tracker or movement manager spinning
description:
After running for a while (~30-60 min), the agent process spikes to 200%+ CPU and stops processing audio. The VAD/STT pipeline freezes because the CPU is saturated.

## Symptoms:
- Process shows 200-280% CPU usage
- No new STT/User: log entries
- Process is still running, not crashed
- Restart fixes it temporarily

## Likely cause:
- InsightFace face detection thread runs continuously with no frame rate limit
- At 640x640 det_size on i7-6700K, each detection takes ~200-300ms
- The detection loop has no sleep — it grabs and processes frames as fast as possible
- Over time this may starve the audio recording thread of CPU

## Possible fixes:
- Add a sleep/rate limit to the face detection loop (e.g., cap at 5 FPS)
- Lower det_size to 320x320 (faster detection, less CPU)
- Add a frame skip when CPU load is high
- Only run face detection when not in active conversation

title: Natural gaze behavior — look at speaker, look away while thinking, look back when responding
description:
The robot should have natural conversational gaze behavior:

1. **While user is speaking**: Look at the speaker (DOA direction) and hold gaze steadily — no spazzing or looking away
2. **While thinking (LLM processing)**: Gently look away a few degrees to the side — mimics human "thinking" behavior  
3. **While responding (TTS playing)**: Look back at the speaker

This is the most natural human conversational pattern. Currently the robot spazzes/looks away erratically during listening due to DOA noise or face tracking jitter.

## Current behavior issues:
- DOA tracking + face tracking can fight each other during conversation
- The thinking animation includes yaw drift which looks like unfocused looking-around rather than deliberate thinking
- No coordination between gaze direction and conversation phase (listening/thinking/speaking)

## Proposed implementation:
- During LISTENING: lock gaze toward DOA direction (or face if visible). Use the spring system but with a longer halflife to prevent jitter. Ignore small DOA changes (apply dead zone to DOA too).
- During THINKING: offset gaze 5-10 degrees to the side from last speaker position. Smooth transition. Keep the subtle thinking animation but smaller/calmer.
- During SPEAKING: smoothly return gaze to speaker direction. Hold steady while TTS plays.

## Relates to:
- robot-tl0 (smooth motion — closed, spring system in place)
- robot-b68 (gaze aversion — this is a more specific version of that)
- DOA tracker already provides speaker direction
- MovementManager already has listening/processing state flags

## Key insight from research:
- Humans look at speakers ~60% of the time with 2.2s average fixation
- Looking away while thinking is a universal human behavior
- The transition timing matters: look away should be slow/gentle, look back should be slightly faster

title: Robot goes to sleep too eagerly — need more explicit trigger
description:
The robot calls go_to_sleep on its own when people don't respond right away, or when it hears casual mentions of sleep/goodbye. This is frustrating — it should wait for a much more explicit command.

## Current behavior:
- LLM calls go_to_sleep tool when it hears "bye", "goodbye", silence, or anything sleep-adjacent
- Tool description says "ONLY use when user directly and explicitly tells you to go to sleep"
- But the 4B model still triggers it too easily

## Possible fixes:
1. **Require confirmation** — go_to_sleep tool asks "Are you sure you want me to sleep?" before actually sleeping
2. **Stronger system prompt** — more explicit instruction not to sleep unless the user says the exact words "go to sleep"
3. **Remove go_to_sleep from tool list entirely** — only allow sleep via the contextual wake detector (which already handles waking)
4. **Add a cooldown** — don't allow sleep within first N minutes of a conversation, or within N turns of last user interaction
5. **Two-step sleep** — LLM expresses intent ("I'm getting sleepy"), orchestrator only triggers actual sleep if user confirms

## Investigation needed:
- Look at conversation logs to see exactly what triggers the false sleep calls
- Check if the system prompt can be made more restrictive
- Consider whether the semantic wake detector's threshold needs tuning too

title: Smooth head motion: critically damped spring + dead zone
description:
Replace EMA-based face tracking with critically damped spring for smoother, calmer head motion.

## Research Findings

Current FACE_EMA_ALPHA=0.25 at 60Hz gives 40ms halflife (saccade speed, 675 deg/s max). Robot looks twitchy/anxious.

### Changes needed:
1. **Critically damped spring** (halflife 0.35s) instead of EMA — smooth acceleration/deceleration, zero overshoot
2. **Dead zone** (2.5°) — ignore micro-jitter from noisy face detection
3. **Velocity clamp** (50 deg/s max) — safety net for sudden jumps
4. **Slower thinking animation** — 1.0s ramp (was 0.5s), 8° amplitude (was 12°), use relative time
5. **Slower antenna smoothing** — alpha 0.15 (was 0.3)
6. **Fix thinking oscillation phase** — use relative time from processing_start, not absolute now

### Spring implementation:
```python
def critically_damped_spring(pos, vel, target, halflife, dt):
    y = (4.0 * 0.693147) / (halflife + 1e-5) / 2.0
    j0 = pos - target
    j1 = vel + j0 * y
    eydt = math.exp(-y * dt)
    new_pos = eydt * (j0 + j1 * dt) + target
    new_vel = eydt * (vel - j1 * y * dt)
    return new_pos, new_vel
```

### Parameter recommendations:
- FACE_SPRING_HALFLIFE = 0.35s (calm but responsive)
- FACE_MAX_VELOCITY = 50 deg/s
- FACE_DEAD_ZONE = 2.5 degrees
- THINKING_RAMP_DURATION = 1.0s (was 0.5)
- THINKING_YAW_AMP = 8 degrees (was 12)
- ANTENNA_ALPHA = 0.15 (was 0.3)

### References:
- theorangeduck.com/page/spring-roll-call — critically damped spring math
- Pollen conversation app uses NO EMA — trusts servo smoothing
- Frontiers in Neurorobotics gaze control paper — 10° dead zone
- Human smooth pursuit: ~30 deg/s max

title: Web dashboard — observe conversations, adjust settings, restart services
description: Build a web dashboard for the Reachy Mini robot. Observe conversations/tool calls in real-time, adjust volume/voice/settings, restart systemd services. User is interested in Go (templ + tailwind) or React. Research needed on best approach.

title: Verbal feedback for slow tool calls — say something while working
description: When the robot calls slow tools (web_search, describe_scene), it should say a brief phrase like 'Let me look that up' or 'Let me check' so the user knows it's working. Currently there's silence during tool execution which feels broken.

title: Body yaw / base rotation — extend range of motion
description:
Add body_yaw (base rotation) support to the animation system.

## Key findings:
- automatic_body_yaw=True is ALREADY enabled (SDK default) — IK auto-rotates base when head looks far sideways
- Physical range: ±160 degrees, max head-body relative angle: 65 degrees
- Pollen's emotions library heavily uses body_yaw (61 of 81 emotions)
- Our emotion_loader.py currently DISCARDS body_yaw from emotion data
- Our AnimationKeyframe has no body_yaw field
- Our set_target call doesn't pass body_yaw

## Implementation:
1. Add body_yaw field to AnimationKeyframe dataclass
2. Pass body_yaw in set_target call (start with 0.0 default)
3. Update emotion_loader.py to extract body_yaw from JSON frames
4. Interpolate body_yaw in keyframe animation (same as antennas)
5. Track current_body_yaw as state in the run loop
6. Reset body_yaw to 0.0 on return-to-center

## Files to modify:
- app/movement_manager.py — dataclass + interpolation + set_target
- app/emotion_loader.py — extract body_yaw from JSON
- app/robot_tools.py — optionally add body_yaw to hand-crafted animations

title: Memory system — short-term and long-term with people profiles
description:
Research and implement a proper memory system for the robot.

## Architecture (from research)

Four tiers, all in SQLite:

**Tier 1: Core Memory (~200 tokens, always in context)**
- Who is present (from face recognition), their key facts, active topic
- Refreshed on person change or topic shift

**Tier 2: People Profiles (retrieved on face recognition)**
- Linked to face embeddings via person_id
- Structured: name, first_seen, last_seen, interaction_count, relationship
- Unstructured facts: preferences, personal details, conversation topics

**Tier 3: Semantic Memory (retrieved on demand)**
- General facts with 384-dim embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, already loaded for wake detection)
- Hybrid search: FTS5 keyword + cosine similarity

**Tier 4: Episodic Memory (existing session_store.py)**
- Full conversation history, already working

## Tools: remember, recall, forget
- remember: save fact with optional person link and category
- recall: hybrid search (FTS5 + embedding similarity)
- forget: remove a specific fact

## Implementation phases:
1. memory_store.py — SQLite schema + hybrid search
2. People profiles — link face_tracker to memory
3. Smart context injection — rebuild system prompt with relevant memories
4. Replace flat memory.md with structured tools
5. Share all-MiniLM-L6-v2 between WakeDetector and MemoryStore

## Token budget (8K context):
- System prompt: ~200 tokens
- Core memory (person + facts): ~200 tokens
- Retrieved memories: ~200 tokens
- Conversation (20 messages): ~6,000 tokens
- Tool schemas: ~800 tokens
- Response: ~600 tokens

## Key decisions:
- No knowledge graph (overkill for <1K memories)
- No separate extraction LLM call (let LLM use remember tool)
- No sqlite-vec (numpy cosine is fast enough for hundreds of vectors)
- Share embedding model instance between wake detector and memory

title: Streaming TTS for Qwen3-TTS GGUF
description:
When switching back to Qwen3-TTS, implement sentence-level streaming to reduce time-to-first-audio.

## Current state:
- LLM streaming is implemented (send_streaming yields sentences)
- Piper gets TTS'd per-sentence (near-instant, works great)
- Qwen3-TTS GGUF has built-in streaming support (TTSConfig.streaming=True)
- The TTS server currently waits for full synthesis before returning

## Plan:
- Qwen3-TTS engine's SpeakerWorker already handles streaming playback internally
- Option A: Add /synthesize_stream endpoint with chunked transfer encoding
- Option B: Load Qwen3-TTS engine in-process (bypass HTTP overhead)
- GPU contention: LLM and TTS talker share GPU, natural serialization per-sentence

## Expected improvement:
- Time-to-first-audio with Qwen3-TTS: ~1.5-3s (currently ~5-6s)

title: Qwen3-TTS voice cloning — British male voice
description:
Set up voice cloning with Qwen3-TTS Base model to create a consistent British male voice for the robot.

## Current state:
- Qwen3-TTS GGUF (1.7B CustomVoice) is set up at /home/david/dev/Qwen3-TTS-GGUF
- Running on GPU via llama.cpp, RTF ~1.2
- Currently using Piper (fast but robotic) while voice is being designed
- VoiceDesign demo available for auditioning voices
- No community British male voice presets exist

## Plan:
1. Use VoiceDesign HuggingFace Space to design ideal British male voice
2. Download the generated audio as reference clip
3. Set up Base model GGUF export (same HaujetZhao pipeline)
4. Clone voice from reference clip, save as voice.json
5. Use cloned voice for all runtime synthesis

## Key info:
- Base model is same 1.7B architecture, same VRAM footprint
- Voice cloning needs 10-15s clean reference audio + transcript
- Accent may drift toward American on longer outputs (known limitation)
- seed pinning keeps voice consistent across generations

title: TTS engine selection — British male voice options
description:
Research on TTS engines with built-in British male voices that fit GTX 1070 (8GB).

## Only two viable options:

**Kokoro (recommended):** 4 British male voices (bm_george, bm_daniel, bm_fable, bm_lewis). 82M params, CPU, ~2-3s/sentence. Top quality. kokoro-onnx variant may be faster.

**Piper (current):** 1 British male (northern_english_male-medium) + alan + VCTK multi-speaker. CPU, sub-second. Lower quality.

## Everything else fails hardware/voice constraints:
- Voxtral: SM 6.1 incompatible, too large
- Parler-TTS: 30s+ on CPU, too slow
- MeloTTS: British voice is female only
- XTTS v2: Clone only, no built-in British
- Edge-TTS: Cloud only
- Qwen3-TTS: No built-in British, needs voice cloning

## Future path:
- Try kokoro-onnx for faster CPU inference
- Qwen3-TTS voice cloning when ready to design a voice
- Revisit when upgrading GPU

title: Gaze aversion — natural look-away behavior
description:
Add human-like gaze aversion so the robot doesn't stare continuously at faces.

## Research findings:
- Humans look at speakers ~60% of the time, with 2.2s average fixation
- Mutual eye contact only 3.5% of conversation time
- Robot should look away every 2-5s for 0.5-2s, then return
- Look-away should be subtle (10-15 degrees down or to the side)
- Pollen/Kuri use similar patterns for natural-feeling interaction

## Implementation:
- Add gaze hold timer (2-5s random) in MovementManager
- During look-away, shift face tracking offset slightly
- Return gaze slightly faster than departure (asymmetric)
- Disable during active conversation (when robot is speaking or listening)

Depends on: robot-tl0 (smooth motion) — gaze aversion needs the spring system to look natural

value: When user asks to add a beads issue: 1) Create a stub issue with bd create to track it immediately. 2) Usually kick off a background agent to research the topic thoroughly — existing codebase, online best practices, HuggingFace/Pollen demos, how to integrate with existing code. 3) The research should be documented in the issue description or comments. 4) This is not just a toy — aim for production quality that serves as a platform for ROS/LLM experimentation. 5) Always investigate Pollen demo repos and Reachy Mini SDK patterns.
For ad-hoc browser testing of the dashboard, use: pnpx @anthropic-ai/playwright-mcp@latest --url http://dave-droid:3001 — lets you visually inspect the page without a full test suite
